title: Guide to integrated data research through the group project pathway | Stats NZ
content:
## Guide to integrated data research through the group project pathway
30 August 2022, 12:13pm
The group project pathway is a newly developed approach that makes data from the IDI more accessible to groups of organisations with related research interests.
This can be a good option for organisations that are keen to partner with others to access integrated data insights and/or lack the technical skills or funding to conduct IDI research independently.
It enables a team of organisations with related research interests to apply for a shared IDI project and pool resources, contracting a third-party researcher to conduct work in a Stats NZ Data Lab.
Stats NZ has created this pathway to reduce the following key barriers to integrated data insights.
- IDI research requires advanced technical skills. Many organisations that could benefit from integrated data insights do not have this expertise in house.
- Hiring an IDI researcher is expensive. This pathway allows multiple organisations to pool funds to hire a researcher together.
- Applying to access the IDI is complicated. Stats NZ is aware that many organisations accessing the group project pathway will have little background knowledge about integrated data, and we are here to provide extra guidance and support along the way.
This page provides a step-by-step guide to accessing the group project pathway to conduct integrated data research for the public good. If you have questions along the way, please don’t hesitate to reach out to us at access2microdata@stats.govt.nz
The diagram below is an overview of the group project pathway process:
Text alternative for Group project pathway diagram
- Phase 1: Assembling the project team
- Phase 2: Preparing to apply
- Phase 3: Applying to conduct IDI research
- Phase 4: After your project is approved

## Phase 1: Assembling the project team

### Finding collaborators
A group project pathway team will be made up of several organisations with a shared interest in collaborating on a research topic that requires integrated data.
The research topic may be broad, with multiple subtopics corresponding to each organisation’s research questions and outcomes of interest. These subtopics will usually require at least some of the same datasets. We are happy to help you determine if the subtopics you want to explore would be appropriate for the group project pathway.
We recommend networking and conducting outreach to connect with teams at public, private, and/or non-profit organisations to assemble a research team. If you are interested in an integrated data research topic but lack the network to assemble a group project pathway team around this topic, we may be able to help you link up with other organisations we work with, or organisations in other Data Lab researchers’ networks.
Please reach out to us for assistance by emailing access2microdata@stats.govt.nz with:
- ‘ Group project pathway ’ in the subject line of your email
- your organisation name
- your research topic and tentative research questions of interest
- any relevant information on desired timelines, etc.

### Scheduling an information session
We understand that integrated data and the IDI may be new concepts to some of the potential collaborators that you connect with. If this is the case, we invite you to schedule an optional introductory information session with our team at Stats NZ that is tailored to your group.
To schedule a session with us and help us to ensure that it meets your group’s needs, please email access2microdata@stats.govt.nz with:
- ‘ Group project pathway ’ in the subject line of your email
- the names of participating organisations and contact information for interested attendees
- your broad research topic and tentative subtopics of interest
- a brief explanation of attendees’ level of knowledge about integrated data and the IDI
- any relevant scheduling restrictions.

## Phase 2: Preparing to apply

### Identifying a project lead
Once your group project pathway team is confirmed, the next step is to identify a project lead. This person will be from one of the participating organisations and will serve as the key liaison between your project team and Stats NZ. They will also be ensuring that the team meets requirements for consultation with communities and dissemination as part of the commitment to research for the public good.
We recommend choosing a project lead based on their level of research experience and understanding, as well as their availability to take on this liaison role.

### Preparing your project brief
The project brief is an important step in the group project pathway that allows the organisations on your team to align on the scope of the research and define the subtopics of interest. It will serve as the basis for discussion at your group’s pre-application meeting with our team.
On the project brief form, you will be asked to compile information from all organisations on your team for submission to Stats NZ, including:
- a list of participating organisations and contact names and email addresses
- an overview of your broad research topic
- each organisation’s specific research objectives
- key dates or desired timeframes.
Download the project brief here:
- Project brief – group project pathway Word document, 40 KB

### Project brief – group project pathway
Word document, 40 KB

### Attending a pre-applicating meeting
Once your project brief is complete, we will arrange a pre-application meeting to discuss your proposed project. This should be attended by key contacts from each participating organisation, including the project lead.
The meeting is an opportunity for your team to meet with us to share your tentative project plans, ask questions, and receive feedback and advice on:
- project feasibility and suitability for the group project pathway
- what datasets may be most relevant for your proposed research
- hiring a researcher to conduct Data Lab work on behalf of your team
- tentative plans for Ngā Tikanga Paihere and publishing your findings
- how to develop the information on your project brief into a successful Data Lab application.
Note: Ngā Tikanga Paihere is the Stats NZ framework for safe, responsible, and culturally appropriate use of data. You will be asked to demonstrate how your project will meet requirements as part of your application, and the project lead will be responsible for ensuring that this is implemented during the course of your research.
This framework may be new to your team, and we are keen to help you understand how to apply it to your project. To enable us to best assist you during the pre-application meeting, please start by familiarising yourself with the following information and include tentative ideas for applying Ngā Tikanga Paihere in the last section of the project brief.
- How to apply Ngā Tikanga Paihere to microdata research projects
Once your brief is ready to share, please schedule your pre-application meeting by emailing access2microdata@stats.govt.nz with:
- ‘ Group project pathway ’ in the subject line of your email
- your completed project brief
- any relevant scheduling restrictions.
Group project pathway teams have the opportunity to pool funds from multiple organisations to hire a skilled integrated data researcher who will work in the Stats NZ Data Lab on their behalf.
We strongly recommend that the researcher you hire has at least intermediate SQL coding skills. Most researchers code in SAS or SQL, while others prefer R or Stata. User support is not provided for statistical software packages.
There are several possibilities for hiring a researcher, including:
- from within one organisation on your team, if it is a larger organisation with integrated data research capability
- from a private research agency in New Zealand
- from the Stats NZ Customised Data Requests team.
While some teams might liaise with potential researchers prior to the pre-application meeting, having a researcher lined up for this initial meeting is not required. Stats NZ will be happy to discuss what kind of researcher would be most appropriate for your project during the meeting, so that you can proceed with this after you meet with us.

## Phase 3: Applying to conduct IDI research

### Drafting your application
The project brief and discussion at the pre-application meeting will serve as the basis for completing the full application form.
Most likely, the project lead and the researcher you hired will collaborate to complete the application form, with input from the organisations on your team.
As you work on your application, the Stats NZ team is happy to meet with you to provide feedback or advice. Please reach out to access2microdata@stats.govt.nz with any questions you come across. If you would like to set up an additional pre-application meeting, write us with:
- ‘ Group project pathway ’ in the subject line of your email
- your draft application form.
Download the application form here:
- Microdata access in the Stats NZ Data Lab: Group project pathway Word document, 293 KB

### Microdata access in the Stats NZ Data Lab: Group project pathway
Word document, 293 KB
The exemplar form below provides guidance along with examples of applications to help you complete the application form (please note that at this point, only single-organisation project examples are included):
- Microdata access application form exemplar – October 2021 Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 1 MB

### Microdata access application form exemplar – October 2021
Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 1 MB

### Submitting your application
Once your application is ready, please email it to access2microdata@stats.govt.nz with:
- ‘ Group project pathway application ’ in the subject line of your email
- your completed application form.
We will be in touch if there are any changes required before your application is finalised and ready for review. We are happy to work with you to refine your draft and can always schedule a follow-up meeting to assist your team if needed.
The application process runs on a six-week cycle. Applications received before the deadline for a cycle will be processed in that batch. While we can occasionally process out of cycle applications, typically applications received after the deadline will go into the following batch.
Tuesday 8 February to Friday 18 March
Tuesday 8 February to Friday 18 March
Monday 21 March to Friday 6 May
Monday 21 March to Friday 6 May
Monday 9 May to Friday 17 June
Monday 9 May to Friday 17 June
Monday 20 June to Friday 29 July
Monday 20 June to Friday 29 July
Monday 1 August to Friday 9 September
Monday 1 August to Friday 9 September
Monday 12 Sept to Friday 21 October
Monday 12 Sept to Friday 21 October
Tuesday 25 October to Friday 2 December
Tuesday 25 October to Friday 2 December
Stats NZ charges an application fee of $500 + GST to cover costs. This fee will be waived if the organisation of your team's project lead is a government agency. There is no charge for unsuccessful applications.

### Review and approval process
The review process may take up to six weeks, but it is likely to be less. We will keep you regularly updated with progress on your application.
Stats NZ subject-matter, legal, and methodology teams will assess your team’s application. Data suppliers (government agencies that submit data to be added to the IDI) may also review applications to use the specific datasets that they supply. Final approval of applications rests with the Government Statistician.

## Phase 4: After your project is approved

### Conducting research in the Stats NZ Data Lab
Getting set up in the Stats NZ Data Lab will require the researcher conducting work on behalf of your team, as well as any other team members requesting Data Lab access, to:
- attend a virtual Data Lab induction session
- attend a virtual confidentiality training session
- sign a contract and related forms.
Stats NZ will then set up access requirements in our systems to provide access to a physical Data Lab and the virtual Data Lab environment to the designated people on your team.
Data Lab rooms are available at all Stats NZ offices (Wellington, Auckland, and Christchurch) and at selected government agencies and universities. Bookings are required for all Data Lab visits. Stats NZ provides both phone and email support to researchers working in the Data Lab, and we share frequent communications and updates with our Data Lab users over email.
The Data and Statistics Act 2022 requires us to protect the confidentiality of the microdata we hold. The Stats NZ Data Lab is a secure environment, meaning that all output and code must be checked by Stats NZ before it is released from the Data Lab to ensure that the confidentiality rules have been applied. Data Lab researchers will typically submit output for checking multiple times over the course of the project.

### Publishing research findings
As part of the requirement that all integrated data research be for the public good, Stats NZ expects organisations who conduct IDI research to publish project findings and share them with us, so we can link to them from our research database. We can also help to promote your research.
We will help you make initial plans for publication and discuss your questions on this during the pre-application meeting, and we are keen to support you with dissemination after your project is complete.
Please share your published research with us or reach out to us about promotion by emailing access2microdata@stats.govt.nz

## Text alternative for Group project pathway diagram
The group project pathway flowchart begins with an IDI project idea. Phase 1 is assembling the project team. It includes these steps: outreach to potential collaborators, information session (optional). Phase 2 is preparing to apply. It includes these steps: identify project lead, prepare project brief, pre-application meeting, hire a researcher. Phase 3 is applying to conduct IDI research. It includes these steps: draft application, submit application, review and approval. Phase 4 is after your project is approved. It includes these steps: conduct research, publish findings.
Page updated 2 September 2022
